name: Gehenna
description: Gehenna, also known as the Valley of Hinnom, is a term originating from the Hebrew Bible as a physical location near Jerusalem, a valley where some Israelites historically practiced child sacrifice to pagan gods. Over time, particularly in Jewish apocalyptic literature and later in Christian theology, Gehenna evolved into a symbolic representation of hell, a place of eternal punishment and suffering for the wicked. It is frequently associated with fire, brimstone, and unimaginable torment, serving as a stark warning against sin and a reminder of the consequences of rejecting divine law. The transition from a geographical site of abhorrent practices to a theological concept of eternal damnation highlights the evolving moral and spiritual understanding within Judaism and Christianity. Gehenna is often contrasted with Paradise or Heaven, representing the ultimate separation from God and the ultimate destination for those deemed unworthy. Its fiery imagery and association with suffering have deeply impacted Western culture, influencing art, literature, and moral philosophy for centuries. It remains a potent symbol of divine justice and the final reckoning.
subtitle: A place of eternal punishment and suffering.
location: Metaphorical; traditionally located near Jerusalem, but now understood as a spiritual realm.
significance: Represents the ultimate consequence of sin and separation from God in Jewish and Christian theology.

keyMyths.title: The Valley of Hinnom and Child Sacrifice
keyMyths.description: The earliest associations with Gehenna stem from the historical practice of child sacrifice to deities like Moloch in the Valley of Hinnom. This abhorrent practice led to the valley becoming associated with impurity, defilement, and divine wrath. This historical context sets the stage for the later symbolic meaning of Gehenna as a place of punishment.

keyMyths.title: Gehenna in the Gospels
keyMyths.description: In the Gospels, Jesus uses the term Gehenna to describe the fiery and eternal punishment awaiting those who reject God's will. He warns against the dangers of sin and the eternal consequences of unrepentance, employing the imagery of Gehenna to illustrate the severity of divine judgment. The Gospels thus solidify Gehenna's place as a central concept in Christian eschatology.

keyMyths.title: Gehenna in Jewish Apocalyptic Literature
keyMyths.description: Jewish apocalyptic texts such as the Book of Enoch expand on the concept of Gehenna, portraying it as a place of torment and fiery judgment for the wicked. These texts contribute to the development of Gehenna as a complex and multifaceted symbol of divine retribution and the ultimate destiny of the unrighteous.

sources.author: David Flusser
sources.description: Explores the Jewish origins and development of the concept of Gehenna, tracing its evolution from a physical location to a theological concept.
sources.text: Explores the Jewish origins and development of the concept of Gehenna, tracing its evolution from a physical location to a theological concept.

sources.author: Bernhard Lang
sources.work: Heaven and Hell: A History of the Afterlife
sources.description: Provides a comparative analysis of afterlife beliefs, including the concept of Gehenna, across various cultures and religions.
sources.text: Provides a comparative analysis of afterlife beliefs, including the concept of Gehenna, across various cultures and religions.

sources.author: Alan F. Segal
sources.work: Life After Death: A History of the Afterlife in Western Religion
sources.description: Examines the development of afterlife beliefs in Western religion, including the evolution of the concept of Gehenna in Judaism and Christianity.
sources.text: Examines the development of afterlife beliefs in Western religion, including the evolution of the concept of Gehenna in Judaism and Christianity.
